title: Plumbing, Heating, and Air-Conditioning Contractors

narrative.executive_view: A lower-middle-market plumbing/HVAC contractor is a credible AI value-retention target, but the value pool is narrow rather than transformational. Most payroll belongs to technicians, installers, helpers, and field supervisors whose physical work AI cannot perform. The useful prize is the office layer: call handling, dispatch, scheduling, pricebook upkeep, estimates, invoicing, documentation, and selected management reporting. Residential service and replacement operators using flat-rate pricebooks can retain a meaningful portion of those savings, while competitively bid commercial and new-construction work will pass more through. Paid demand is durable because repair and installation remain physical and, for refrigerant work, federally certification-gated [S2].
narrative.how_ai_changes_work: AI changes the coordination around the wrench, not the wrench work itself. ServiceTitan's 2025 survey found 46% of more than 1,000 contractors using or experimenting with AI, with administration the leading use area at 59%, marketing and sales at 51%, and customer communication and field operations at 39% [S1]. That is consistent with automating CSR scripts, booking, dispatch suggestions, job summaries, estimate follow-up, invoicing, and permit paperwork. ServiceTitan's pricebook product also exposes AI-supported price comparisons, equipment-document retrieval, troubleshooting guidance, and automated job notes [S8]. These tools raise technician utilization but do not eliminate licensed installation, diagnosis in the building, quality control, or customer-facing accountability.
narrative.value_capture: Residential service/replacement commonly uses an upfront flat-rate pricebook; ServiceTitan states that flat-rate systems dominate among the HVAC contractors it supports [S8]. Because the customer buys an outcome rather than hours, an operator can initially keep much of an administrative productivity gain. Capture is not complete: local competitors buy the same software, dynamic local price comparisons can re-anchor prices, lead-generation costs can absorb savings, and commercial/new-construction work remains bid-intensive. The underwriting therefore assigns strong distribution retention but only a middling moat. Regulatory and physical-work barriers support terminal demand, not the capture score.
narrative.adoption_timing: The broad survey signal is already substantial, but 'using or experimenting' is not the same as effective deployment across target workflows. The packet discounts ServiceTitan's 46% headline to an estimated 30% current effective adoption for the dominant residential/light-commercial service segment and places the 50% threshold about three years away. Embedded features are the likely path: 59% of AI users in the survey reported using features already built into existing software [S1]. Fragmented small shops, poor process data, integration work, and technician/dispatcher trust slow the conversion from experimentation to labor removal.
narrative.consolidation_economics: The market is unquestionably active: Capstone counted 149 announced or completed HVAC-services transactions through September 2025 and documented Sila, TruTemp, Liberty Service Partners, NexCore, and other acquirers [S5]. Size-tiered valuation evidence matters more than headline transactions. Western Commerce's February 2026 table places residential businesses at 8.4x EBITDA for $1-5 million EBITDA and 9.9x for $5-10 million, with lower commercial-service multiples [S4]. The packet uses 8.0x entry and 9.5x exit to reflect a mixed-code target that can become a scaled, service-heavy platform, while recognizing that contingent consideration, owner dependence, normalization, and technician retention can erase the spread. BizBuySell's much smaller HVAC transactions average 2.75x seller's discretionary earnings, confirming that micro-shop SDE multiples cannot be mixed mechanically with lower-middle-market EBITDA [S3].
narrative.terminal_demand: Terminal demand is durable. HVAC and plumbing require an on-site person, tools, parts, and responsibility for building systems; EPA Section 608 requires certification for technicians maintaining, servicing, repairing, or disposing of covered refrigerant equipment [S2]. AI may reduce avoidable visits through remote diagnostics and may shift the customer relationship toward OEMs or national platforms, but it does not install a heat pump, replace a compressor, repair a drain line, or accept job-site liability. PHCC also describes continued skilled-worker demand amid retirements and a tight pipeline [S9]. Those mechanisms preserve paid field demand while simultaneously capping the AI-addressable wage share.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The code is heterogeneous: residential service/replacement, commercial maintenance, and mechanical new-construction subcontracting have different margins, pricing, cyclicality, and AI capture. The deterministic labor share is an ancestor-level, mixed-vintage low-quality input, and the target count uses an engineering/construction margin that likely misses profitable 20-99 employee service firms. AI adoption evidence is a vendor-sponsored, cross-trade survey and does not measure labor removed. Multiple evidence is current and size-tiered but still advisory and directional. Founder dependence, license transfer, technician scarcity, customer concentration, seasonality, and the mix of recurring maintenance agreements are acquisition-specific risks that cannot be settled at the NAICS level.

sources.title: Majority of Contractors Already Seeing Increased Efficiency From AI Adoption, ServiceTitan Industry Report Reveals
sources.what_it_supports: Survey of more than 1,000 contractors: 46% using or experimenting with AI, functional use areas, embedded-software adoption path, and survey limitations.

sources.title: Section 608 Technician Certification
sources.what_it_supports: Federal certification requirement for technicians maintaining, servicing, repairing, or disposing of covered refrigerant equipment.

sources.title: HVAC Business Valuation Multiples & Financial Benchmarks
sources.what_it_supports: Reported small HVAC sales for 2021-2025, including 2.75x average and 2.58x median seller-discretionary-earnings multiples; establishes a micro-business basis distinct from EBITDA.

sources.title: HVAC & Plumbing Services M&A Report — February 2026
sources.what_it_supports: Directional private-company EBITDA multiples by $500K-$1M, $1M-$5M, and $5M-$10M EBITDA tiers and residential/commercial/industrial type; operational and licensing risks.

sources.title: HVAC Services M&A Coverage Report — December 2025
sources.what_it_supports: 2025 deal volume, named active acquirers and add-on strategies, fragmentation, and the distinction between large disclosed transaction multiples and private lower-middle-market targets.

sources.title: Voice of Contractor Highlight: HVACR Contractors Are Getting Younger
sources.what_it_supports: 2024 HVACR contractor survey age distribution: 30% age 55-64 and 23% age 65+, with sample and purchasing-decision context.

sources.title: FMI Releases 2024 Study on Ownership Transfer and Management Succession
sources.what_it_supports: Built-environment succession proxy: 58% lacked an ownership transition plan and half of owners planning to exit in three to five years lacked one.

sources.title: HVAC Flat-Rate Pricing Template
sources.what_it_supports: Flat-rate pricebooks dominate among ServiceTitan-supported HVAC contractors; documents estimate, invoicing, pricebook automation, and field AI workflows.

sources.title: PHCC Releases 2026 Industry Environmental Scan Highlighting Key Trends and Opportunities
sources.what_it_supports: Exact-trade association evidence on skilled-talent demand, retirements, a tight worker pipeline, rising AI exploration, and continuing consolidation.

ai_replaceable_share.rationale: Role-by-role task judgment limits field trades to documentation and diagnostic support, gives office and sales workflows much higher fractions, and assigns the 26.49% residual a blended 30% fraction. The resulting Python-weighted share is about 16.4%; licensing and physical presence bound the core trades while observed AI use concentrates in administration, sales, communication, and workflow support.
caveats: The role mix is at five-digit 23822 rather than exact 238220 and leaves a 26.49% residual whose composition is uncertain.
Vendor survey use cases indicate task direction, not measured labor displacement.

role_judgments.rationale: Physical licensed pipe installation and repair dominate; AI assists documentation, code lookup, and diagnostics only.
On-site HVAC installation and service persist; manuals, troubleshooting, and job notes are the limited automatable slice.
Scheduling, reporting, and job-cost administration automate, while on-site crew leadership and quality control remain.
Booking, data entry, invoice preparation, permit paperwork, and routine communications are highly workflow-automatable.
Fabrication and installation are physical; digital takeoff and documentation are a small task slice.
Reporting, pricing analysis, and coordination automate substantially, but personnel, vendor, customer, and operating accountability stay human.
Material handling and assistance at the job site are physical; training and paperwork are a minimal automatable slice.
Dispatch coordination and progress reporting automate, but technical escalation, inspection, and field leadership remain.
The role is overwhelmingly physical assistance with only a small documentation/training component.
Lead qualification, quote generation, follow-up, and financing paperwork automate; complex in-home or account closing remains human.
Residual blends estimators, dispatchers, CSRs, bookkeepers, permit staff, project managers, and unlisted field trades; high office exposure is diluted by physical roles.

pi_dist.rationale: Flat-rate residential service prices are not mechanically tied to labor hours, allowing initial retention of office savings; competitive bids and shared software pass more savings through in commercial/new-construction work.
caveats: The value assumes a residential/light-commercial service-heavy operator; commercial construction would be lower.

pi_moat.rationale: Local brand, service agreements, dispatch density, and certifications provide some protection, but AI and pricebook software are widely purchasable and buyer competition is active.
caveats: Licensing supports service availability and demand but is not treated as a complete barrier to price competition.

t50_years.rationale: Embedded vendor features and substantial experimentation can move effective adoption from an estimated 30% to 50% within roughly three years, tempered by fragmented operations and integration/trust constraints.
caveats: Survey usage is cross-trade and includes experimentation rather than scaled labor removal.

current_adoption_pct.rationale: ServiceTitan reports 46% using or experimenting with AI; discounting experimentation and non-material uses yields an estimated 30% of firms with effective use in at least one target workflow.
caveats: Vendor-sponsored survey of multiple residential and commercial trades is not representative of exact NAICS 238220.

historical_analogs.value: Field-service-management and flat-rate-pricebook digitization: embedded workflow tools diffuse through vendors, first standardizing dispatch, estimates, invoices, and reporting before changing field labor.
historical_analogs.rationale: The closest observable analogue is the industry's migration from manual office processes to integrated field-service and pricebook software, because AI is arriving inside the same workflow layer.
historical_analogs.plausible_range: Rapid office adoption over 2-5 years; limited direct displacement of field trades
caveats: The analogue is qualitative and does not identify a causal labor-reduction rate.

selection.rationale: HARDI reports 30% of surveyed HVACR contractors age 55-64 and 23% age 65+; interpolating the 60-64 portion produces about 42% age 60+, rounded for selection bias.
caveats: HARDI survey respondents are contractors/purchasing decision-makers, not a probability sample of exact-code firm owners; plumbing coverage is incomplete.

succession_shortage_documented.rationale: FMI documents widespread absent transition plans across engineering/construction, while PHCC documents retirements and a tight worker pipeline in plumbing/heating/cooling; together they support a real succession-capacity shortage.
succession_shortage_documented.plausible_range: true; false if exact-code owner succession is required
caveats: FMI covers the broader built environment and PHCC's shortage statement concerns skilled talent, so the exact-code owner-succession bridge is a disclosed proxy.

active_consolidators.rationale: Conservative count of named 2025 acquirer/platform activity directly documented by Capstone: Sila, TruTemp/Centre-Baldwin, Liberty Service Partners/NorthCurrent, NexCore/Trinity Hunt, and Sterling Infrastructure.
caveats: The count is a documented minimum, not a census, and includes commercial HVAC as well as residential home services.

buy_mult.rationale: Western's current directional table reports 8.4x for $1-5M EBITDA residential firms and 7.6x for commercial firms; 8.0x reflects a mixed service operator. BizBuySell's 2.75x SDE average is retained only as a smaller-business basis cross-check.
caveats: Western's table is illustrative and partly synthesized; BizBuySell is SDE, mostly below the target EBITDA band, and is not converted into EBITDA.

exit_mult.rationale: Western reports 9.9x for $5-10M EBITDA residential firms and 8.8x for commercial firms; a 9.5x blended exit assumes scale, recurring service revenue, professional management, and reduced owner dependency without importing 14x large disclosed transaction averages.
caveats: Achieving the selected exit requires mix and quality improvement; deal structures and market rates can reduce realized cash value.

pricing_structure.value: Residential service/replacement is predominantly upfront flat-rate pricebook pricing; commercial maintenance mixes contracts and time/material, while new construction is competitively bid.
pricing_structure.rationale: ServiceTitan explicitly states flat-rate systems dominate among the HVAC contractors it supports and documents different estimating tools for home service, known-scope projects, and complex commercial work.
pricing_structure.plausible_range: Flat-rate dominant in residential service; bid/contract pricing dominant in commercial and construction
caveats: No exact-NAICS revenue-weighted pricing-model survey was located; plumbing and commercial-mechanical mixes may differ.

cross_checks.uplift_vs_A_consistent: A roughly 16% replaceable task share is meaningful but bounded, and a three-year path to 50% effective adoption is consistent with current experimentation concentrated in office workflows rather than field substitution.

terminal_value.justification: Paid demand for on-site installation, maintenance, and repair survives AI; physical execution, building responsibility, and refrigerant certification remain, even if diagnostics and customer acquisition shift upstream.

cross_checks.no_proxy_confirmed: No-proxy is not confirmed universally: EPA certification is direct for covered refrigerant work, while ownership age, succession, pricing mix, and parts of the code boundary rely on disclosed HVAC or broader-construction proxies.
cross_checks.pricing_model_deflation: Flat-rate residential pricing delays mechanical pass-through, but common software, local bidding, online price transparency, lead costs, and PE-scaled competitors can re-anchor pricebooks; commercial/new-construction savings should pass through faster.

dataset_mismatch.description: Labor share is ancestor-level 23822 with 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and low quality; role mix is five-digit; n_band uses a broad engineering/construction margin and likely omits profitable 20-99 employee service targets.
sensitivity: A higher exact-code labor share raises V proportionally.
If residential service margins put many 20-99 employee firms in the EBITDA band, buyability is materially understated by n_band=1,152.
A construction-heavy target has lower capture and lower multiples than the selected service-heavy case.

market_boundary.selection_basis: This is the dominant roll-up target model by firm count and the segment to which the current pricing and transaction evidence most directly applies; commercial/new-construction exposure is treated as downside heterogeneity.

top_uncertain_inputs.score_impact: Moves adoption score from 5.0 to 10.0 within the stated range.
Materially changes buyability because the only age evidence is an HVACR respondent proxy.
Changes raw labor-value opportunity by about four percentage points of revenue at the range endpoints.
The multiple-expansion score can be near zero or roughly two depending on entry quality, scale, and market conditions.

reviewer_note: Evaluate the owner-age interpolation, broader-construction succession bridge, and the applicability of Western's directional size-tier multiples to a mixed plumbing/HVAC target.
